retrieved_docs.title: Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation

Langfuse is an open-source platform for tracing, evaluating, and monitoring LLM applications. It captures full traces of agent runs including prompts, completions, tool calls, and latency. Langfuse integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and OpenAI SDK, and supports human annotation workflows for building evaluation datasets. It is used by teams to detect regressions, measure hallucination rates, and optimize prompt quality.

retrieved_docs.title: Composio: Tool Integration Layer for AI Agents
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Composio: Tool Integration Layer for AI Agents

Composio provides a managed integration layer that connects LLM agents to over 250 external tools and APIs (GitHub, Slack, Notion, Jira, etc.) with built-in authentication handling. It simplifies the tool-use layer of agentic pipelines, letting developers focus on agent logic rather than API plumbing. Composio integrates natively with CrewAI, LangChain, and AutoGen.

retrieved_docs.title: Weaviate Blog — weaviate-agent-skills
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Weaviate Blog — weaviate-agent-skills

It has never been easier to build software. With tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot, we’ve moved into a high-velocity workflow often called ‘vibe coding.’ You describe a feature, an agent blueprints the logic, and the app comes to life.
But this speed usually hits a wall when it meets specialized infrastructure. While agents are great at general boilerplate, they struggle with the nuances of working with specialized tools. They often hallucinate legacy v3 Weaviate syntax, guess at hybrid search alpha parameters, or fail to implement efficient multivector embedding strategies. When you’re trying to manage three parallel agentic workflows, you don't have time to be a full-time debugger for an agent’s hallucinations. You need the implementation to be right on the first try.
To solve this, we are introducing Weaviate Agent Skills. This repository acts as a bridge between the most popular coding agents and Weaviate’s infrastructure.
Agent Skills has become one of the most popular tools to help coding agents write better code. The Agent Skills format, developed by Anthropic, works with Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, Gemini CLI, and many other tools. Our new

retrieved_docs.title: Cursor Blog — cursorbench
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Cursor Blog — cursorbench

How we compare model quality in Cursor
Developers are asking coding agents to take on longer, more complex tasks that span multiple files, tools, and steps. As these requests grow in scope, the evals that measure agent performance need to evolve with them.
At Cursor, we use a hybrid online-offline eval process to keep our understanding of model quality aligned with what developers actually do.
The offline part uses CursorBench, our internal eval suite based on real Cursor sessions from our engineering team. Because tasks come from actual Cursor usage rather than public repositories, CursorBench is both better at distinguishing between models and more aligned with real developer outcomes than public benchmarks.
We built CursorBench to measure multiple dimensions of agent performance including solution correctness, code quality, efficiency, and interaction behavior. This blog focuses on solution correctness results, but in practice we evaluate agents across all of these axes.
We supplement CursorBench with controlled analysis on live traffic. These online evals catch regressions that offline suites miss, like where the agent's output looks correct to a grader but feels worse to a dev

retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: LangGraph: Stateful Agent Orchestration with Graphs
retrieved_docs.snippet:
LangGraph: Stateful Agent Orchestration with Graphs

LangGraph, built on top of LangChain, provides a graph-based abstraction for building reliable, stateful LLM agents. Developers define nodes (agent steps) and edges (transitions) in a directed graph, enabling cyclic workflows, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and fine-grained state management. LangGraph is widely used for production-grade agentic pipelines requiring persistence and controllability.

retrieved_docs.title: Tavily Blog — accelerate-ai-development-databricks-discover-govern-and-build-mcp-and-agent-bricks
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Tavily Blog — accelerate-ai-development-databricks-discover-govern-and-build-mcp-and-agent-bricks

Connect your AI agents to a growing ecosystem of governed external services via the new MCP Catalog and Databricks Marketplace
by Elise Gonzales, Nikhil Gaekwad and Harish Gaur
Data intelligence is transforming how enterprises operate, allowing organizations to build AI agents that reason over vast amounts of proprietary data. A critical component of this evolution is the ability for AI agents to connect to external services and third-party data assets to take real-world actions. This is where the Model Context Protocol (MCP) shines, acting as a standardized way for LLMs to access external tools.
However, leveraging MCP at scale presents three major enterprise challenges:
Databricks now addresses these challenges with a secure, unified, and scalable MCP experience within Agent Bricks: the MCP Catalog (Beta) for discovery and governance, an MCP in Databricks Marketplace (Public Preview) to access a growing ecosystem of external tools, and integration with the Supervisor Agent (Beta) to enable agentic orchestration.
This launch, part of Databricks’ Week of Agents, advances our vision for AI that accurately reasons on your data. By unifying governance, discovery, and real-time action

retrieved_docs.title: Anyscale Blog — announcing-anyscale-agent-skills-ray
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Anyscale Blog — announcing-anyscale-agent-skills-ray

Today we're announcing the general availability of Anyscale Agent Skills, equipping popular AI coding agents with the Ray and Anyscale expertise needed to write, deploy, debug, and optimize workloads at scale. Agent Skills are distributed as a first-party feature of the Anyscale CLI, and are available today on Claude Code and Cursor.
Three skill groups, generally available today, support the full development lifecycle from generating workloads to deploying and debugging in production:
Workload Skills → Generate code and configs for LLM serving, multimodal data processing, distributed training, batch inference, and embedding generation
Platform Skills → Debug, fix, and redeploy live workloads using logs, metrics, and managed workspaces
Infra Skills → Deploy Ray on Kubernetes or VMs with guided, production-ready setup
Working together in sync, Workload and Platform Skills achieves 5x development speed compared to general-purpose agents like Claude Code and Cursor.
Get started by installing these skills via the Anyscale CLI:
pip install -U anyscale
anyscale login
anyscale skills install --platform claude-code
# or
anyscale skills install --platform cursor

retrieved_docs.title: Microsoft AutoGen: Conversational Multi-Agent Framework
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Microsoft AutoGen: Conversational Multi-Agent Framework

AutoGen (by Microsoft Research) is a framework for building LLM applications where multiple agents communicate via structured conversations. It supports customizable agent roles, code execution, and tool use. AutoGen v0.4 introduces a layered architecture with Core, AgentChat, and Extensions, enabling modular and extensible agent systems for coding assistants, research automation, and enterprise workflows.
